subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Two blocks of masses $2$ kg and $1$ kg respectively, are tied to the ends of a string which passes over a light frictionless pulley as shown in the figure below. The masses are held at rest at the same horizontal level and then released. The distance traversed by the centre of mass in $2$ s is _______ m. (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
question: Two blocks of masses $2$ kg and $1$ kg respectively, are tied to the ends of a string which passes over a light frictionless pulley as shown in the figure below. The masses are held at rest at the same horizontal level and then released. The distance traversed by the centre of mass in $2$ s is _______ m. (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
The acceleration of the blocks in the Atwood machine is given by:

$a = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1 + m_2} g$

Substituting the given values ($m_1 = 2$ kg, $m_2 = 1$ kg):

$a = \dfrac{2 - 1}{2 + 1} g = \dfrac{g}{3}$

Taking the downward direction as positive, the acceleration of the $2$ kg block is $a_1 = \dfrac{g}{3}$ and the acceleration of the $1$ kg block is $a_2 = -\dfrac{g}{3}$.

The acceleration of the centre of mass is:

$a_{cm} = \dfrac{m_1 a_1 + m_2 a_2}{m_1 + m_2}$

$a_{cm} = \dfrac{2 \left(\dfrac{g}{3}\right) + 1 \left(-\dfrac{g}{3}\right)}{2 + 1} = \dfrac{\dfrac{g}{3}}{3} = \dfrac{g}{9}$

Since the system is released from rest, the initial velocity of the centre of mass is zero.

The distance traversed by the centre of mass in $t = 2$ s is:

$S_{cm} = \dfrac{1}{2} a_{cm} t^2$

$S_{cm} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{10}{9}\right) (2)^2 = \dfrac{20}{9} \approx 2.22 \text{ m}$

Answer: $2.22$

q: A piece of wood of mass <math><mn>0.03</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is dropped from the top of a <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> height building. At the same time, a bullet of mass <math><mn>0.02</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is fired vertically upward, with a velocity <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> from the ground. The bullet gets embedded in the wood. Then the maximum height to which the combined system reaches above the top of the building before falling below is: <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: A piece of wood of mass <math><mn>0.03</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is dropped from the top of a <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> height building. At the same time, a bullet of mass <math><mn>0.02</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is fired vertically upward, with a velocity <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> from the ground. The bullet gets embedded in the wood. Then the maximum height to which the combined system reaches above the top of the building before falling below is: <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
Using relative velocity



arel=0

vrel=100

Time t for collision,

100=vrel×t

t=100100=1 sec

vbullet=100-1×10=90m/s

vparticle=10×1=10m/s

S=100×1-12×10×1=95 m



Pi=Pf

⇒90×0.02-10×0.03=0.05V

V=30m/s⇒h=v22g=90020=45 m

So from top of building

45-5=40 m

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> moving at speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> collides elastically with a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> at rest. After the collision, the two masses move at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> with respect to the initial direction of motion of the body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mo>.</mo></math>. The largest possible value of the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>M</mi><mi>m</mi></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> for which the angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be equal, is :
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> moving at speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> collides elastically with a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> at rest. After the collision, the two masses move at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> with respect to the initial direction of motion of the body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mo>.</mo></math>. The largest possible value of the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>M</mi><mi>m</mi></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> for which the angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be equal, is :
solution:
Linear momentum conservation
(i) y-direction
mv2sinθ=mv1sinθ
mv2=mv1           ...(1)
(ii) x-direction
mv0=mv2cosθ+mv1cosθ            ...(2)
from (1) and (2)
mv0=mv2cosθ+mv2cosθ
mv0=2mv2cosθ       ...(3)
(iii) e=1given
v2-v1cos2θv0cosθ=1
v2-v1cos2θ=v0cosθ       ...(4)
v2-m22Mcos2θ=v0cosθ
v21-mMcos2θ=v0cosθ
mv02mcosθ1-mMcos2θ=v0cosθ
M-mcos2θ=2mcos2θ
M=2mcos2θ+m2cos2θ-1
Mm=4cos2θ-1
Mmmax=4-1=3

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A uniform rod of mass 250 g having length 100 cm is balanced on a sharp edge at 40 cm mark. A mass of 400 g is suspended at 10 cm mark. To maintain the balance of the rod, the mass to be suspended at 90 cm mark, is
question: A uniform rod of mass 250 g having length 100 cm is balanced on a sharp edge at 40 cm mark. A mass of 400 g is suspended at 10 cm mark. To maintain the balance of the rod, the mass to be suspended at 90 cm mark, is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \tau_{\text {Net }}=0 \Rightarrow(400 \mathrm{~g} \times 30)=(250 \mathrm{~g} \times 10)(\mathrm{mg} \times 50) \\ & \mathrm{m}=\frac{12000-2500}{50}=\frac{9500}{50} \\ & \mathrm{M}=190 \mathrm{~g}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Four particles $A, B, C, D$ of mass $\frac{m}{2}, m, 2 m, 4 m$, have same momentum, respectively. The particle with maximum kinetic energy is :
question: Four particles $A, B, C, D$ of mass $\frac{m}{2}, m, 2 m, 4 m$, have same momentum, respectively. The particle with maximum kinetic energy is :
solution:
$\mathrm{KE}=\frac{\mathrm{p}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}}$
Same momentum, so less mass means more KE. So $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{2}$ will have max. $\mathrm{KE}$.

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Consider two blocks A and B of masses $m_1=10 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_2=5 \mathrm{~kg}$ that are placed on a frictionless table. The block A moves with a constant speed $v=3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ towards the block B kept at rest. A spring with spring constant $\mathrm{k}=3000 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is attached with the block B as shown in the figure. After the collision, suppose that the blocks A and B, along with the spring in constant compression state, move together, then the compression in the spring is, (Neglect the mass of the spring)
question: Consider two blocks A and B of masses $m_1=10 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_2=5 \mathrm{~kg}$ that are placed on a frictionless table. The block A moves with a constant speed $v=3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ towards the block B kept at rest. A spring with spring constant $\mathrm{k}=3000 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is attached with the block B as shown in the figure. After the collision, suppose that the blocks A and B, along with the spring in constant compression state, move together, then the compression in the spring is, (Neglect the mass of the spring)
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{v}_2=\left(\mathrm{m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2\right) \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{cm}} \\ & \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{cm}} \Rightarrow \frac{10 \times 3}{10+5} \Rightarrow \frac{30}{15}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{kx}^2=\frac{1}{2}(10)(3)^2-\left[\frac{1}{2}(15)(2)^2\right]$
$\Rightarrow 90-60=30=3000 x^2$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{x}^2 \Rightarrow \frac{30}{3000}=\frac{1}{100} \\ & \mathrm{x} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{10} \mathrm{~m}.\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q:
This question has Statement - I and Statement - II  of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.

Statement - I: A point particle of mass m moving with speed ν collides with stationary point particle of mass M. If the maximum energy loss possible is given as
f12mν2 then f=mM+m.

Statement - II: Maximum energy loss occurs when the particles get stuck together as a result of the collision.
question:
This question has Statement - I and Statement - II  of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.

Statement - I: A point particle of mass m moving with speed ν collides with stationary point particle of mass M. If the maximum energy loss possible is given as
f12mν2 then f=mM+m.

Statement - II: Maximum energy loss occurs when the particles get stuck together as a result of the collision.
options: Statement-<math><mrow><mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math> is true, Statement-<math><mrow><mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is false.
Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math> is false, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is true.

Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math> is true, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is true, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is a correct explanation of Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math>.

Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math> is true, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is true, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is not a correct explanation of Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math>.
solution:
Maximum energy loss will take place in perfectly inelastic collision, and is given by, ∆K=P22m-P22m+M
⇒∆K=P22m1-mm+M
⇒∆K=P22mMm+M
⇒∆K=12mv2Mm+M
On comparing with the given form of equation, we have, f=Mm+M

Hence Statement- I is false, Statement- II is true. ( As it is perfect Inelastic collision)

q: A rubber ball is released from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the floor. It bounces back repeatedly, always rising to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>81</mn><mn>100</mn></mfrac></math> of the height through which it falls. Find the average speed of the ball. (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A rubber ball is released from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the floor. It bounces back repeatedly, always rising to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>81</mn><mn>100</mn></mfrac></math> of the height through which it falls. Find the average speed of the ball. (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
v0=2gh

v=e2gh=2gh

⇒e=0.9

S=h+2e2h+2e4h+………

t=2h g+2e2h g+2e22h g+………

vav=st=2.5 m s-1

q: The mass of a hydrogen molecule is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3.32</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27 </mn></mrow></msup><mi>kg</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup></math> hydrogen molecules strike, per second, a fixed wall of the area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> at an angle of <math><msup><mrow><mn>45</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> to the normal, and rebound elastically with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then the pressure on the wall is nearly:
question: The mass of a hydrogen molecule is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3.32</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27 </mn></mrow></msup><mi>kg</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup></math> hydrogen molecules strike, per second, a fixed wall of the area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> at an angle of <math><msup><mrow><mn>45</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> to the normal, and rebound elastically with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then the pressure on the wall is nearly:
solution:
F=2mnVcosθ⇒P=2mnVcosθA

⇒P=2×3.32×10-27×1023×1032×2×10-4

⇒P=2.35×103 N m-2

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ falls freely from a height of $100 \mathrm{~m}$, on a platform of mass $3 \mathrm{~kg}$ which is mounted on a spring having spring constant $\mathrm{k}=1.25 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. The bodysticks to the platform and the spring's maximum compression is found to be $x$. Given that $g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2},$ the value of $\mathrm{x}$ will be close to :
question: A body of mass $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ falls freely from a height of $100 \mathrm{~m}$, on a platform of mass $3 \mathrm{~kg}$ which is mounted on a spring having spring constant $\mathrm{k}=1.25 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. The bodysticks to the platform and the spring's maximum compression is found to be $x$. Given that $g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2},$ the value of $\mathrm{x}$ will be close to :
solution: Velocity of $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ block just before it collides with $3 \mathrm{~kg}$ block $=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{gh}}=\sqrt{2000} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ Using principle of conservation of linear momentum just before and just after collision, we get $1 \times \sqrt{2000}=4 \mathrm{v} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\frac{\sqrt{2000}}{4} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ Initial compression of spring $1.25 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{x}_{0}=30 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}_{0} \approx 0$ using work energy theorem, $\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{g}}+\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\Delta \mathrm{KE}$ $\Rightarrow 40 \times x+\frac{1}{2} \times 1.25 \times 10^{6}\left(0^{2}-x^{2}\right)$ $=0-\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times v^{2}$ solving $x \approx 4 \mathrm{~cm}$

q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Body P having mass M moving with speed u has head-on collision elastically with another body Q having mass m initially at rest. If m≪M, body Q will have a maximum speed equal to 2u after collision.

Reason R : During elastic collision, the momentum and kinetic energy are both conserved.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Body P having mass M moving with speed u has head-on collision elastically with another body Q having mass m initially at rest. If m≪M, body Q will have a maximum speed equal to 2u after collision.

Reason R : During elastic collision, the momentum and kinetic energy are both conserved.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: A is correct but R is not correct.
A is not correct but R is correct.
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
solution:
For e=1 & second body at rest

V2=2m1u1 m1+m2=2uMM+m≃2u

Since M>>m

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A uniform rod <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and Length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> at rest on a smooth horizontal surface. An impulse of force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is applied to end B. The time taken by the rod to turn through at right angles will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> ____.
question: A uniform rod <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and Length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> at rest on a smooth horizontal surface. An impulse of force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is applied to end B. The time taken by the rod to turn through at right angles will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> ____.
solution:
Impulse J=0.2 N s

⇒J=∫Fdt=0.2 N s

Now, angular impulse (M→) will be

Mc=∫τdt

=∫FL2dt

=L2∫Fdt=L2×J

=0.32×0.2

=0.03

Moment of inertia of the rod about the centre of mass, Icm=ML212=2×(0.3)212=0.096

Angular impulse will be equal to the change in angular momentum.

M=Icmωf-ωi

⇒0.03=0.096ωf

⇒ωf=2 rad s-1

For angular displacement, we can write θ=ωt

⇒t=θω=π2×2=π4 s.

Therefore, x=4.

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Four particles A, B, C  and D with masses mA=m,mB=2m,mC=3m  and mD=4m are at the corners of a square. They have accelerations of equal magnitude with directions as shown. The acceleration of the centre of mass of the particles is:
question: Four particles A, B, C  and D with masses mA=m,mB=2m,mC=3m  and mD=4m are at the corners of a square. They have accelerations of equal magnitude with directions as shown. The acceleration of the centre of mass of the particles is:
solution:
a→A=-ai^
a→B=aj^
a→C=ai^
a→D=-aj^
a→cm=maa→a+mba→b+mca→c+mda→dma+mb+mc+md
a→cm=-mai^+2maj^+3mai^-4maj^10m

=2mai^-2maj^10,=a5i^-a5j^=a5i^-j^

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> collides with another particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> which is at rest initially. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the velocities of particles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> after collision respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and after collision <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>−</mo><mfenced><mrow><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the angle between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is :
question: Particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> collides with another particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> which is at rest initially. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the velocities of particles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> after collision respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and after collision <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>−</mo><mfenced><mrow><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the angle between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is :
solution:
m1u→1+m2u→2=m1v→1+m2v→2

2m2(3i^+j^)+m2×0=2m2(i^+3j^)+m2×v→2

23i^+2j^=2i^+23j^+v→2

v→2=(3-1)i^-(3-1)j^

v→1=i^+3j^

Angle between v→1 and v→2 is 105°

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: In a perfectly inelastic collision, two spheres made of the same material with masses 15 kg and 25 kg, moving in opposite directions with speeds of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, respectively, strike each other and stick together. The rise in temperature (in ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$), if all the heat produced during the collision is retained by these spheres, is : (specific heat of sphere material $31 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{kg}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1 \mathrm{cal}=4.2 \mathrm{~J}$)
question: In a perfectly inelastic collision, two spheres made of the same material with masses 15 kg and 25 kg, moving in opposite directions with speeds of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, respectively, strike each other and stick together. The rise in temperature (in ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$), if all the heat produced during the collision is retained by these spheres, is : (specific heat of sphere material $31 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{kg}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1 \mathrm{cal}=4.2 \mathrm{~J}$)
solution:
Using conservation of momentum:
$m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2 = (m_1 + m_2)v_f$ where $v_2 = -30$ m/s (opposite direction).
$15(10) + 25(-30) = 40v_f \Rightarrow v_f = -15$ m/s
Initial kinetic energy:
$KE_i = \frac{1}{2}(15)(10)^2 + \frac{1}{2}(25)(30)^2 = 750 + 11250 = 12000$ J
Final kinetic energy:
$KE_f = \frac{1}{2}(40)(15)^2 = 4500$ J
Heat produced:
$Q = 12000 - 4500 = 7500$ J $= \frac{7500}{4.2} = 1785.71$ cal
Temperature rise:
$\Delta T = \frac{Q}{m_{total} \cdot c} = \frac{1785.71}{40 \times 31} = 1.44°C$

question: A body of mass $m=3.513 \mathrm{~kg}$ is moving along the $x$-axis with a speed of $5.00 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The magnitude of its momentum is recorded as
solution:
$$
P=m v=3.513 \times 5.00 \approx 17.6
$$

q: The position vector of the center of mass r→cm  of an asymmetric uniform bar of negligible area of cross-section as shown in figure is:
question: The position vector of the center of mass r→cm  of an asymmetric uniform bar of negligible area of cross-section as shown in figure is:
solution:
The position vector of center of mass r→cm is given as 

r→cm=Xcmx^+Ycm y^

Where, Xcm=x-coordinate of center of mass  and Ycm=y-coordinate of center of mass

r→cm=m1x1+m2x2+m3x3m1+m2+m3 x^ +m1y1+m2y2+m3y3m1+m2+m3 y^

r→cm=2m×L+m×2L+m5L22m+m+mx^+2m×L+m×L2+m×02m+m+my^

r→cm=13L8x^+5L8y^

q: A man of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is running on the road and suddenly jumps into a stationary trolly car of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. Then the trolly car starts moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The velocity of the running man was _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, when he jumps into the car.
question: A man of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is running on the road and suddenly jumps into a stationary trolly car of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. Then the trolly car starts moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The velocity of the running man was _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, when he jumps into the car.
solution:
Applying conservation of the momentum,

mmu=mm+mtv⇒60u=60+120×2⇒u=6 m s-1

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The center of mass of a thin rectangular plate (fig - x ) with sides of length $a$ and $b$, whose mass per unit area $(\sigma)$ varies as $\sigma=\frac{\sigma_0 x}{a b}$ (where $\sigma_0$ is a constant), would be $\qquad$
question: The center of mass of a thin rectangular plate (fig - x ) with sides of length $a$ and $b$, whose mass per unit area $(\sigma)$ varies as $\sigma=\frac{\sigma_0 x}{a b}$ (where $\sigma_0$ is a constant), would be $\qquad$
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & d m=\sigma d A \\ & \begin{aligned} d & =\sigma(d x)(d y)=\frac{\sigma_0 x}{a b}(d x)(d y) \\ x_{c o m} & =\frac{\int x d m}{\int d m}=\frac{\int x \frac{\left(\sigma_0 x\right)}{a b}(d x)(d y)}{\int_0 \frac{\sigma_0}{a b}(d x)(d y)} \\ & =\frac{\int_0^a x^2 d x \int_0^b d y}{\int_0^b x d x \int_0^b d y}=\frac{2 a}{3} \\ y_{c o m} & =\frac{\int y d m}{\int d m}=\frac{\int y\left(\frac{\sigma_0 x}{a b}\right)(d x)(d y)}{\int \frac{\sigma_0 x}{a b}(d x)(d y)}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
$=\frac{\int_0^a x d x \int_0^b y d y}{\int_0^a x d x \int_0^b d y}=\frac{b}{2}$
i.e., $\vec{r}_{\mathrm{com}} \equiv\left(\frac{2 a}{3}, \frac{b}{2}\right)$

question: A body having specific charge 8 μC g-1 is resting on a frictionless plane at a distance 10 cm from the wall (as shown in the figure). solution:
F=ma
qE=ma
a=qEm
Now d=12at2
t=2 da
t=2 dqEm

t=2×0.18×10-610-3×100=12
∴ Time period =2t=1 s
Answer =1.00

q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> makes an elastic collision with another body at rest and continues to move in the original direction but with one fourth of its initial speed. question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> makes an elastic collision with another body at rest and continues to move in the original direction but with one fourth of its initial speed. The speed of the two body centre of mass is $x/10$. Find the value of x.
solution:
pi=pf

2×4=2×1+m2×v2

m2v2=6 …(1)

By coefficient of restitution, 
1=v2-14⇒v2=5 m s-1

by (i)

m2×5=6

m2=1.2 kg

vcm=m1v1+m2v2 m1+m2

vcm=2×1+1.2×52+1.2=83.2=2510

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass 14 kg initially at rest explodes and breaks into three fragments of masses in the ratio $2: 2: 3$. The two pieces of equal masses fly off perpendicular to each other with a speed of $18 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ each. The velocity of the heavier fragment is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
question: A body of mass 14 kg initially at rest explodes and breaks into three fragments of masses in the ratio $2: 2: 3$. The two pieces of equal masses fly off perpendicular to each other with a speed of $18 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ each. The velocity of the heavier fragment is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
From mass ratio 2:2:3 with total mass 14 kg: individual masses are 4 kg, 4 kg, and 6 kg.
Setting coordinate axes along the motion directions of the two equal fragments: fragment 1 has momentum $\vec{p_1} = 4 \times 18\hat{i} = 72\hat{i}$ kg⋅m/s, and fragment 2 has $\vec{p_2} = 4 \times 18\hat{j} = 72\hat{j}$ kg⋅m/s.
By momentum conservation, initially at rest: $\vec{p_1} + \vec{p_2} + \vec{p_3} = 0$, so $\vec{p_3} = -72\hat{i} - 72\hat{j}$ kg⋅m/s.
Velocity of 6 kg fragment: $\vec{v_3} = \frac{\vec{p_3}}{6} = -12\hat{i} - 12\hat{j}$ m/s.
Speed: $|\vec{v_3}| = \sqrt{144 + 144} = \sqrt{288} = 12\sqrt{2}$ m/s.

q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext mathvariant="italic">m</mtext></math> moving in the <math><mi>x</mi></math> direction with speed <math><mn>2</mn><mi>v</mi></math> is hit by another particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi></math> moving in the <math><mi>y</mi></math> direction with speed <math><mi>v</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the collision is perfectly inelastic, the percentage loss in the energy during the collision is close to:
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext mathvariant="italic">m</mtext></math> moving in the <math><mi>x</mi></math> direction with speed <math><mn>2</mn><mi>v</mi></math> is hit by another particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi></math> moving in the <math><mi>y</mi></math> direction with speed <math><mi>v</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the collision is perfectly inelastic, the percentage loss in the energy during the collision is close to:
solution:
The initial momentum of system is Pi→=m2Vi^+2mV j^

According to question as



On perfectly inelastic collision the particles stick to each other.

Pf→=3m Vf→

By conservation of linear momentum

Pf→=Pi→ ⇒3m Vf→=m2V i^+2mVj^

⇒Vf→=2V3i^+j^⇒ | V f | =223V

∴ loss in KE. of system =Kinitial-Kfinal

=12m2V2+122mV2-123m 2 2 V⁡ 3 2

=2mV2+mV2-43mV2=3mV2-4mV23=53mV2

% Loss in KE =100×∆KKi=53mv23mV2=59×100=56%

question: A rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> has non-uniform linear mass density given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mi>l</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math><mi>a</mi></math> and <math><mi>b</mi></math> are constants and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>l</mi></math> The value of <math><mi>x</mi></math> for the centre of mass of the rod is at:

=ax220ι+bι2x440ιax0ι+bι2x330ι
=aι22+bι24aι+bι3=2a+b3a+bι4×3
=3ι42a+b3a+b

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A bullet of 10 g leaves the barrel of gun with a velocity of 600 m s-1. If the barrel of gun is 50 cm long and mass of gun is 3 kg, then value of impulse supplied to the gun will be:
question: A bullet of 10 g leaves the barrel of gun with a velocity of 600 m s-1. If the barrel of gun is 50 cm long and mass of gun is 3 kg, then value of impulse supplied to the gun will be:
solution:
The formula to calculate the impulse J on the gun is given by

J=mv-u   ...1

where, m is the mass, u is the initial velocity and v is the final velocity of the bullet.

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required impulse.

J=10 g×1 kg1000 g×600-0 m s-1= 6 N s

q: A rod of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is lying on a horizontal frictionless surface. A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> travelling along the surface hits at one end of the rod with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> in a direction perpendicular to the rod. The collision is completely elastic. After collision, particle comes to rest. The ratio of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>m</mi><mi>M</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be
question: A rod of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is lying on a horizontal frictionless surface. A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> travelling along the surface hits at one end of the rod with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> in a direction perpendicular to the rod. The collision is completely elastic. After collision, particle comes to rest. The ratio of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>m</mi><mi>M</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be
solution:
From momentum conservation, Pi0=Pf
mu=Mv …i
From angular momentum conservation about O,
mu·L2=ML212ω
⇒ω=6muML …(ii)
From e=R.V·SR.V·A
1=V+ωL2u

V+ωL2=u
V+3muM=u
muM+3muM=u
4muM=u
mM=14
x=4

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The block of mass $M$ moving on the frictionless horizontal surface collides with a spring of spring constant $\mathrm{K}$ and compresses it by length $\mathrm{L}$. The maximum momentum of the block after collision is
question: The block of mass $M$ moving on the frictionless horizontal surface collides with a spring of spring constant $\mathrm{K}$ and compresses it by length $\mathrm{L}$. The maximum momentum of the block after collision is
solution: $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{KL}^2=\frac{\mathrm{P}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}} \quad \therefore \mathrm{P}=\sqrt{\mathrm{MK}} \mathrm{L}$
